title: Helm Cheat Sheet
description: 80+ Helm commands for install, upgrade, rollback, values, templating, repositories, and debugging. Includes a Go Template Quick Reference. Verified against official Helm docs.

goTemplateRef.title: Go Template Quick Reference
goTemplateRef.subtitle: The most confusing Helm templating patterns and the exact errors they fix. These are the issues that generate the most Stack Overflow questions and Reddit complaints about Helm.

patterns.title: Whitespace Control
patterns.description: The hyphen trims whitespace and newlines on that side of the expression. Use {{- to trim whitespace before the tag and -}} to trim whitespace after it.
patterns.errorFixed: Unexpected blank lines or extra indentation in rendered YAML
patterns.gotcha: Missing a hyphen on one side only trims that side. Forgetting both hyphens leaves extra blank lines that can break YAML parsing in strict validators.

patterns.title: nindent vs indent
patterns.description: nindent adds a newline before the indented content. indent does not add a newline. Use nindent when embedding a multi-line value inside a YAML block.
patterns.errorFixed: YAML indentation errors when embedding multi-line values
patterns.gotcha: Using indent instead of nindent when the value starts on the same line as the key causes the first line to be unindented relative to the rest producing invalid YAML.

patterns.title: toYaml for complex values
patterns.description: toYaml converts a Helm values object into a YAML string. Always pipe it through nindent to get correct indentation.
patterns.errorFixed: nil pointer evaluating interface {} when rendering nested objects
patterns.gotcha: Forgetting nindent after toYaml produces a single-line YAML string with no indentation which is invalid in nested contexts.

patterns.title: required for mandatory values
patterns.description: required fails the helm install or upgrade with a clear error message if the specified value is empty or not set. Use this to enforce required values instead of letting them fail silently.
patterns.example:
image:
  tag: {{ required "image.tag must be set" .Values.image.tag }}
patterns.errorFixed: Silent deployment failures when required values are missing
patterns.gotcha: Without required a missing value renders as an empty string which may cause the pod to fail at runtime with a confusing error rather than failing at install time with a clear message.

patterns.title: default for fallback values
patterns.description: default provides a fallback value when the specified value is empty, nil, or not set in values.yaml.
patterns.errorFixed: nil pointer evaluating interface {} when a value is not set
patterns.gotcha: default only activates when the value is empty or nil. If the user explicitly sets a value to false or 0 those are treated as set values and default does not activate.

patterns.title: Nil Pointer Error
patterns.description: Always check if a parent key exists before accessing a child key. Accessing .Values.section.key when section is not defined causes a nil pointer error.
patterns.gotcha: This is the single most common Helm template error. Always wrap nested value access in an if block that checks the parent key first.

commands.title: Install a chart as a new release

flags.description: Creates the namespace if it does not exist
Overrides a single value on the command line
Simulates the install without making changes

commands.useCase: Install a Helm chart into your Kubernetes cluster with a release name you choose.
commands.beginnerNote: The release name you choose must be unique within the namespace. If a release with that name already exists, use helm upgrade instead.

commands.title: Install or upgrade a release idempotently

flags.description: Automatically rolls back if the upgrade fails
Waits until all pods are running before returning

commands.useCase: Install a chart if it does not exist or upgrade it if it does. This is the idempotent command used in CI pipelines.
commands.beginnerNote: Always use --atomic in production upgrades so Helm automatically rolls back if the new version fails to deploy. Without --atomic, a failed upgrade leaves the release in a broken pending-upgrade state.

commands.title: List installed releases
commands.useCase: List all Helm releases installed in the current namespace.
commands.beginnerNote: helm list without -A only shows releases in the current namespace set in your kubectl context. If you cannot find your release, add -A to search all namespaces.

commands.title: Roll back a release to a previous revision

flags.description: Waits until all pods are running after rollback
Simulates the rollback without making changes

commands.useCase: Roll back a release to a previous revision number when an upgrade causes problems.
commands.beginnerNote: Run helm history my-release to see all revision numbers before running rollback. Revision 0 means the previous revision.

commands.title: Uninstall a release

flags.description: Keeps the release history in Kubernetes secrets after uninstalling

commands.useCase: Remove a Helm release and all Kubernetes resources it created.
commands.beginnerNote: Uninstall deletes all resources created by the chart including PersistentVolumeClaims by default. If you want to keep your data, add --keep-history and handle PVC deletion manually.

commands.title: Create a new chart scaffold
commands.useCase: Create a new Helm chart with the standard directory structure and example templates.
commands.beginnerNote: The generated chart includes example deployment, service, and ingress templates that you modify for your application. Delete the templates you do not need rather than leaving empty files.

commands.title: Package a chart into a .tgz archive

flags.description: Sets the output directory for the packaged chart

commands.useCase: Package a chart directory into a .tgz archive for distribution or uploading to a chart repository.

commands.title: Show a chart's default values
commands.useCase: Display all the default values for a chart so you know what you can override in your values file.
commands.beginnerNote: Pipe the output to a file with helm show values bitnami/nginx > values.yaml to create a starting point for your custom values file.

commands.title: Show a chart's metadata
commands.useCase: Display the chart metadata including version, description, and dependencies.

commands.title: Show a chart's README
commands.useCase: Display the README for a chart to understand how to configure and use it.

commands.title: Update chart dependencies
commands.useCase: Download and update all chart dependencies listed in Chart.yaml before packaging or installing.
commands.beginnerNote: Run this before helm install or helm package when working with a chart that has dependencies. Forgetting this step causes missing subchart errors.

commands.title: List chart dependencies
commands.useCase: List all dependencies for a chart and their current download status.

commands.title: Download a chart locally

flags.description: Sets where to save the chart

commands.useCase: Download a chart from a repository to inspect or modify it locally.

commands.title: Simulate an install without applying it
commands.useCase: Simulate an install and see the rendered Kubernetes manifests without applying anything to the cluster.
commands.beginnerNote: --dry-run requires a connection to the cluster to validate the manifests. Use helm template instead for a fully offline render.

commands.title: Upgrade an existing release

flags.description: Waits for pods to be ready
Forces resource updates by deleting and recreating
Cleans up new resources if upgrade fails

commands.useCase: Upgrade an existing release to a new chart version or with updated values.
commands.beginnerNote: Without --atomic, a failed upgrade leaves the release in pending-upgrade state which blocks future upgrades until you manually roll back.

commands.title: Upgrade with a single value override
commands.useCase: Upgrade a release and override a single value without a full values file.
commands.beginnerNote: Values set with --set take precedence over values files but are not stored anywhere persistent. Document overrides in a values file for reproducibility.

commands.title: Roll back to the previous revision
commands.useCase: Roll back to the immediately previous revision using revision number 0 as shorthand.

commands.title: Show a release's revision history

flags.description: Limits the number of revisions shown

commands.useCase: Show the complete revision history of a release including status, chart version, and description for each revision.
commands.beginnerNote: Revision numbers in history are what you use with helm rollback. If history shows no entries, the release may have been installed with --no-hooks or history may have been purged.

commands.title: Install and wait for resources to be ready
commands.useCase: Install a chart and wait until all pods, PVCs, and services are in a ready state before returning success.
commands.beginnerNote: Without --wait, helm returns immediately after submitting the manifests to Kubernetes even if pods fail to start. Add --wait in CI so a failed deploy is caught at install time.

commands.title: Upgrade and reset values to chart defaults
commands.useCase: Upgrade a release and reset all values back to the chart defaults, discarding any custom values from previous installs.
commands.beginnerNote: This discards all your custom configuration. Use --reuse-values instead if you want to keep existing overrides and only change specific values.

commands.title: Upgrade while keeping previous values
commands.useCase: Upgrade a release while keeping all the values from the previous install and only overriding specific ones.

commands.title: Install with an auto-generated release name
commands.useCase: Install a chart with an automatically generated release name, useful in CI environments where you want unique release names.

commands.title: Uninstall while keeping release history
commands.useCase: Remove all resources created by a release while keeping the release history so you can roll back if needed.

commands.title: Install a specific chart version
commands.useCase: Install a specific version of a chart instead of the latest, important for reproducible deployments.
commands.beginnerNote: Without --version, helm installs the latest chart version available in the repo index, which can silently introduce breaking changes between environments if not pinned.

commands.title: Install or upgrade with a pinned version and values
commands.useCase: Combine the idempotent upgrade or install pattern with a pinned chart version and custom values, the standard production CI deploy command.

commands.title: Preview a rollback before running it
commands.useCase: Preview what a rollback to a specific revision would change before actually performing it.

commands.title: Preview an uninstall before running it
commands.useCase: Preview which resources would be deleted by an uninstall without actually removing anything.

commands.title: Force resource replacement during upgrade
commands.useCase: Force resource updates through a replace strategy, deleting and recreating resources that cannot be updated in place.
commands.beginnerNote: --force can cause brief downtime since it deletes resources before recreating them. Avoid using it as a routine upgrade flag and reserve it for resources stuck in an unpatchable state.

commands.title: Extend the install wait timeout
commands.useCase: Extend how long Helm waits for resources to become ready before considering the install failed, useful for charts with slow-starting dependencies.

commands.title: Limit the number of retained revisions
commands.useCase: Limit how many old revisions Helm retains for a release, keeping only the most recent ones to reduce clutter in the release secrets.

commands.title: Install without running lifecycle hooks
commands.useCase: Install a chart without running any of its lifecycle hooks, useful when a hook is failing and you need to get the base resources deployed.
commands.beginnerNote: Skipping hooks can leave a chart partially configured since many charts rely on hooks for jobs like database migrations. Only use this as a temporary debugging measure.

commands.title: Uninstall without running lifecycle hooks
commands.useCase: Uninstall a release without running any pre-delete or post-delete hooks.

commands.title: Render chart templates locally

flags.description: Renders only a specific template file
Sets the namespace in rendered output

commands.useCase: Render chart templates locally to see the exact Kubernetes YAML that would be applied without connecting to the cluster.
commands.beginnerNote: helm template does not validate against a live cluster so it can render manifests that are invalid in your specific cluster. Use helm install --dry-run for server-side validation.

commands.title: Render templates to a file
commands.useCase: Render all templates to a single YAML file for review or for applying with kubectl apply -f.

commands.title: Lint a chart for errors

flags.description: Uses a custom values file for linting

commands.useCase: Check a chart for common errors and best practice violations before packaging or installing.
commands.beginnerNote: helm lint catches many template issues but not all. Always follow with helm template to visually inspect the rendered output.

commands.title: Show the values used for a release

flags.description: Shows values from a specific revision

commands.useCase: See the custom values that were used to install or upgrade a release.
commands.beginnerNote: helm get values without --all only shows values that were explicitly overridden. Add --all to see the complete computed values including chart defaults.

commands.title: Show the manifests applied for a release
commands.useCase: Show the Kubernetes manifests that were applied to the cluster for a release.

commands.title: Show everything about a release
commands.useCase: Show all information about a release including values, manifests, hooks, and notes.

commands.title: Install with a custom values file
commands.useCase: Install a chart using custom values from a file to override the chart defaults.
commands.beginnerNote: Multiple -f flags are supported and values are merged left to right with later files taking precedence.

commands.title: Upgrade with a custom values file
commands.useCase: Upgrade a release with values from a file.
commands.beginnerNote: If you switch from --set overrides to a values file during an upgrade, use --reset-values first or the old --set values may persist.

commands.title: Export default values as a starting point
commands.useCase: Save the chart default values to a file as a starting point for building your custom values file.

commands.title: Show chart metadata, values, and README together
commands.useCase: Display the chart metadata, default values, and README all together in a single command output.

commands.title: Render a single template file
commands.useCase: Render only one specific template file instead of the entire chart, useful when debugging a single resource.

commands.title: Force a value to be treated as a string
commands.useCase: Force a --set value to be treated as a string instead of letting Helm infer its type, important for values like version tags that look numeric.
commands.beginnerNote: Without --set-string, a value like image.tag=1.25 that looks like a number can be parsed as a numeric type instead of a string, which breaks templates that expect a string.

commands.title: Set a value from a file's contents
commands.useCase: Set a value to the contents of a file instead of typing it inline, useful for multi-line configuration blocks or certificates.

commands.title: Add a chart repository
commands.useCase: Add a Helm chart repository so you can install charts from it.
commands.beginnerNote: You must run helm repo update after adding a new repository before you can install charts from it.

commands.title: Refresh chart repository indexes
commands.useCase: Fetch the latest list of charts from all configured repositories.
commands.beginnerNote: Run this regularly or before installing charts to ensure you are seeing the latest available versions.

commands.title: List configured repositories
commands.useCase: List all configured chart repositories and their URLs.

commands.title: Remove a chart repository
commands.useCase: Remove a chart repository from your local configuration.
commands.beginnerNote: Removing a repository does not uninstall any charts already installed from it.

commands.title: Search configured repositories

flags.description: Enables regex matching in the search term

commands.useCase: Search your configured repositories for charts matching a keyword.
commands.beginnerNote: helm search repo only searches repositories you have added with helm repo add. Use helm search hub to search the public Artifact Hub.

commands.title: Search the public Artifact Hub
commands.useCase: Search the public Artifact Hub for publicly available Helm charts.
commands.beginnerNote: Charts found on the hub may be from any publisher. Check the publisher verification status before using a chart in production.

commands.title: Authenticate to an OCI registry
commands.command: helm registry login registry.example.com --username myuser --password mypassword
commands.useCase: Authenticate to an OCI registry to push or pull charts stored as OCI artifacts.
commands.beginnerNote: OCI registry support was added in Helm 3.8 as stable. If you are on an older version, use a traditional chart repository instead.

commands.title: Push a packaged chart to an OCI registry
commands.useCase: Push a packaged chart to an OCI registry.

commands.title: Generate a repository index file
commands.useCase: Generate an index.yaml file for a directory of packaged charts, turning it into a valid Helm chart repository.

commands.title: Download a chart by repository URL
commands.useCase: Download a chart directly by repository URL without first running helm repo add, useful for one-off downloads.

commands.title: Check a release's current status

flags.description: Shows all Kubernetes resources created by the release
Shows the status of a specific revision

commands.useCase: Check the current status of a Helm release including whether it is deployed, failed, or pending.
commands.beginnerNote: helm status shows the release status from Helm's perspective. Use kubectl get pods to see the actual pod status.

commands.title: List failed releases
commands.useCase: List only releases that are in a failed state so you can quickly identify broken deployments.

commands.title: List releases stuck in a pending state
commands.useCase: List releases stuck in pending-install or pending-upgrade state which indicates a previous operation did not complete cleanly.
commands.beginnerNote: A release stuck in pending-upgrade blocks all future upgrades. Fix it by running helm rollback my-release to a known good revision.

commands.title: Show a release's NOTES.txt output
commands.useCase: Display the NOTES.txt output from a chart which usually contains instructions for accessing the deployed application.

commands.title: Show a limited number of recent revisions
commands.useCase: Show the last 5 revisions of a release to understand recent changes.

commands.title: List only healthy deployed releases
commands.useCase: List only releases that are currently in a healthy deployed state, filtering out failed or pending ones.

commands.title: Lint a chart with strict validation
commands.useCase: Lint a chart and treat all warnings as errors for strict validation in CI pipelines.

commands.title: Render templates with debug output
commands.useCase: Render templates with debug output showing which template files are being processed and any errors in detail.

commands.title: Simulate an install with debug output
commands.useCase: Simulate an install with full debug output showing the rendered manifests and all Helm operations.

commands.title: Upgrade with debug output
commands.useCase: Upgrade with full debug output to diagnose upgrade failures.

commands.title: List a release's hook manifests
commands.useCase: List all hook manifests associated with a release to debug pre-install or post-install hook failures.
commands.beginnerNote: Hook failures are a common cause of releases being stuck in pending-install state. Inspect the hook pod logs with kubectl logs after identifying the hook pod name.

commands.title: List installed Helm plugins
commands.useCase: List all installed Helm plugins.

commands.title: Show a release's status with full resource list
commands.useCase: Show the full status of a release along with every Kubernetes resource it created, useful for confirming exactly what got deployed.

commands.title: View the manifest from a past revision
commands.useCase: View the exact manifest that was applied for a specific past revision, useful for comparing what changed between two failed deployments.

commands.title: Preview an upgrade diff against the live cluster
commands.command: helm diff upgrade my-release bitnami/nginx -f values.yaml
commands.useCase: Preview exactly what an upgrade would change in the live cluster before running it for real, using the popular helm-diff plugin.
commands.beginnerNote: This requires installing the helm-diff plugin first with helm plugin install https://github.com/databus23/helm-diff. It is not part of the core Helm CLI.

commands.title: Render and validate templates against the cluster API
commands.useCase: Render templates and validate them against the Kubernetes API schema of the connected cluster, catching invalid API versions or fields.
commands.beginnerNote: --validate requires a connection to a live cluster to check the resources against, unlike a plain offline helm template render.

commands.title: Show NOTES.txt output from subcharts
commands.useCase: Display the NOTES.txt output from subcharts in addition to the parent chart, useful when debugging which chart in a dependency tree is producing confusing output.

commands.title: Confirm you are running Helm 3
commands.useCase: Confirm you are running Helm 3, which does not require Tiller, uses namespaced releases by default, and uses OCI registries.
commands.beginnerNote: Helm 2 required a server-side component called Tiller with cluster-admin permissions, which was a major security concern. Helm 3 removed Tiller entirely and stores release data as Kubernetes secrets in the release namespace.

commands.title: View Helm 3's release state secrets
commands.useCase: View the Kubernetes secrets that Helm 3 uses to store release state in the release namespace.

commands.title: Migrate a Helm 2 release to Helm 3
commands.useCase: Migrate a Helm 2 release to Helm 3 format using the 2to3 plugin.
commands.beginnerNote: Install the plugin first with helm plugin install https://github.com/helm/helm-2to3.

commands.title: Remove leftover Helm 2 data after migration
commands.useCase: Remove all Helm 2 data including Tiller's ConfigMaps after confirming every release has been successfully migrated to Helm 3.
commands.beginnerNote: Only run cleanup after verifying every Helm 2 release was migrated and is working correctly in Helm 3, since cleanup permanently deletes the Helm 2 release data you would need to roll back.

commands.title: Install a Helm plugin
commands.useCase: Install a Helm plugin from a URL.

commands.title: Update an installed plugin
commands.useCase: Update an installed plugin to its latest version.

commands.title: Remove an installed plugin
commands.useCase: Remove an installed plugin.

commands.title: Show Helm environment variables
commands.useCase: Show all Helm environment variables and their current values including plugin directory, data directory, and cache directory.

commands.title: Install a pinned plugin version
commands.useCase: Install a specific pinned version of a plugin instead of the latest, useful for reproducible CI environments.
